clean_story:
Invisible patterns. Equations as flashlights.

The phrase "predicting unseen worlds" isn’t about fortune-telling—it’s math acting like a metal detector on a beach, beeping over buried coins no one can see yet. Take Neptune: In 1846, astronomers noticed Uranus wobbling off-course. Instead of guessing, Urbain Le Verrier wrote equations treating the wobble as a gravitational tug from an unseen planet. His math pointed to a spot in the sky; telescopes looked and found Neptune within a degree of his prediction.

Or consider radio waves. James Clerk Maxwell’s 1860s equations said light was just one type of wave—there should be invisible, longer ones too. Twenty years later, Heinrich Hertz built a spark gap transmitter and detected them, proving math had "seen" radio before humans did.

Even weirder: Paul Dirac’s 1928 equation for electrons predicted antimatter—particles identical but opposite in charge. No one had seen such things. Then, in 1932, Carl Anderson spotted positrons in cosmic rays, matching Dirac’s math like a key in a lock.

Math doesn’t invent these worlds; it listens to the gaps in what we can see, then sketches the shape of what’s missing.

Three related subjects are dark matter calculations, fractals in nature, and how errors expose truths.
